coredata.dc:title: Re-discussion on the essence of the ultra-bright fluorescent carbon dots synthesized by citric acid and ethylenediamine
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Mengna
Long, Xiang
Ma, Yanshun
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: Citric acid and ethylenediamine can synthesize ultrabright fluorescent carbon dots (super CDs, S-CDs) with photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) up to ∼100% by simple hydrothermal method, almost exceeding all the currently reported fluorescent carbon dots. In this work, we examined the synthesis and properties of S-CDs in details. Our observations suggest that carbonization/graphitization degree contributes little to the PLQY of S-CDs. Morphological characterization shows the S-CDs have no graphene cores. Excitation wavelength independent emission behavior implies that the S-CDs might have an exclusive emission center/fluorophore, which is independent of the quantum size effect. Dialysis experiment reveals that not only nanoparticles, small molecular fragments also have PL emission ability. All the above experimental observations suggest that the S-CDs should be not real CDs but copolymer of CA and EDA. Furthermore, a possible reaction mechanism was proposed for synthesis of the S-CDs. This work reveals the essence of S-CDs and paves the way for their large-scale industrial production and applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence mechanism
Quantum yield
Citric acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of relative PLQY and yield

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: PLQY as functions of heating (a) temperature (T), (b) time (t) and (c) molar ratio of CA and EDA (R CA/EDA). Yield (or conversion rate) as functions of heating (c) temperature (T), (d) time (t) and (e) molar ratio of CA and EDA (R CA/EDA).

Morphology and optical properties of S-CDs. (a) Three-dimensional and (b) two-dimensional fluorescence spectra. (c) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra. (d) Normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. (e) Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image. (f) A zoom-in TEM image of a particle in 2 nm resolution.

Other optical behaviors. PL spectra (a) and intensities (b) of S-CDs as functions of concentration of NaCl; PL spectra (c) and intensities (d) of S-CDs as functions of pH; PL spectra (e) and intensities (f) of S-CDs as functions of temperature; PL spectra (g) and intensities (h) of S-CDs as functions of solvent.
Schematic diagrams of dialysis experiment before (a) and after (b) dialysis balance; The integral fluorescence and absorbance (c); PLQYs of the product, retention, and exudate solutions (d).
Possible reaction mechanism of S-CDs synthesized by CA and EDA. The 1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo [1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (IPCA) might be the emission center/fluorophore of the S-CDs.

head.ce:title: Re-discussion on the essence of the ultra-bright fluorescent carbon dots synthesized by citric acid and ethylenediamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Citric acid and ethylenediamine can synthesize ultrabright fluorescent carbon dots (super CDs, S-CDs) with photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) up to ∼100% by simple hydrothermal method, almost exceeding all the currently reported fluorescent carbon dots. In this work, we examined the synthesis and properties of S-CDs in details. Our observations suggest that carbonization/graphitization degree contributes little to the PLQY of S-CDs. Morphological characterization shows the S-CDs have no graphene cores. Excitation wavelength independent emission behavior implies that the S-CDs might have an exclusive emission center/fluorophore, which is independent of the quantum size effect. Dialysis experiment reveals that not only nanoparticles, small molecular fragments also have PL emission ability. All the above experimental observations suggest that the S-CDs should be not real CDs but copolymer of CA and EDA. Furthermore, a possible reaction mechanism was proposed for synthesis of the S-CDs. This work reveals the essence of S-CDs and paves the way for their large-scale industrial production and applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of nano-fluorescent materials [1,2], have attracted extensive research interests since their discovery in 2004 [3]. Due to their advantages of low toxicity, low synthesis cost, and wide source of raw materials [4–7], CDs show a wide application prospect in the fields of display equipment [8–10], energy conversion [11–13], bioimaging [14–16], analytical detection [17–19], etc. However, compared with organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, most fluorescent CDs suffer from low photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY), which seriously hinders their practical applications. In recent years, with the deepening of research, more and more CDs with high PLQY have been developed. For example, Ma, et al. fabricated multi-color CDs from the heavy oil and its solvent deasphalting products, with the maximum PLQY up to 64% [20]. Jia et al. used N,N-dipropyl-P-PD as precursor to synthesize red-emitting CDs with PLQY as high as 86% [21]. Moreover, Qu, et al. used citric acid and ethylenediamine to synthesize a super fluorescent CDs (S-CDs) by a simple hydrothermal method, and the PLQY is up to ∼100% [22], almost exceeding all the currently reported fluorescent CDs. However, the mechanism of photoluminescence remains unclear. In the current work, we studied the synthesis and optical behavior of the S-CDs, and all the observations imply that the S-CDs may not be real CDs, but may be polymer dots with an exclusive fluorophore. Strictly speaking, the S-CDs may still be an organic dye.

Hydrothermal reactor (25 mL) was purchased from Anniu Technology Co., Ltd. Electric drying oven was purchased in Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. UV–vis spectra were measured by a Shunyuhengping 756 PC UV–vis spectrophotometer, and fluorescence spectra were determined by a Shanghai Lengguang F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Quinine sulfate was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Chemical reagents such as citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Tianjin Comiou Chemical reagent Co., Ltd. The experimental water was self-made secondary distilled water.

The synthesis of S-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Typically, 2 mmol CA and 8 mmol EDA are dissolved in 10 mL secondary distilled water and ultrasonicated for 30 min. Then the mixture is loaded into a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene lined hydrothermal reactor and heated for 5 h at 170°. After that, the reactor is cooled naturally. The product solution is firstly filtered with a 0.22 μm microporous filter to remove insoluble solid particles, then loaded into a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 1000 Da) and dialyzed for 48 h. The final solution is stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for standby.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of relative PLQY and yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The relative PLQY of S-CDs is estimated with quinine sulfate as a standard reference. It is suggested that quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution has a PLQY value of 54% [23]. Then one can calculate the relative PLQY of test sample by the following equation,
(1)
PLQY U
=
PLQY S
⋅
F U
F S
⋅
A S
A U
⋅
(
η S
η U
)
2
where the subscripts S and U refer to the standard substance and test sample, respectively; F represents the integral fluorescence intensity; A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength; η denotes the refractive index of solvent. In order to avoid the interference of inner filter effect, the absorbances (A) of both standard substance and test sample should be less than 0.05.

Another important parameter is conversion rate of raw materials, termed yield. In fact, the conversion rate of raw materials can be estimated from the weight of product CDs powder, which could be obtained through a group of operating procedures such as purification, drying and weighing. However, the large number of samples means a huge workload of this determination. On the other hand, incomplete purification or drying may affect the accuracy of measurement results. Here, an effective concentration C ac is defined to estimate the conversion rate or yield,
(2)
C ac
∝
F PLQY
When the total amount of substance of the raw materials is constant, a higher C ac represents a higher conversion rate or yield.

Most fluorescent CDs have a characteristic carbon core structure. Therefore, carbonization/graphitization plays an important role in enhancing PLQY of CDs. Herein, we carefully examined the effects of reaction conditions on the PLQY of S-CDs and the conversion rate (represented by yield) of precursors. Fig.2 a and d shows the effect of temperature (T) on the PLQY and yield of S-CDs respectively. With the increase of temperature, PLQY first increases and then decreases, and the optimum temperature appears around 170 °C. On the other hand, a relatively high yield (∼23%) of precursors is also observed at near 170 °C. Fig. 2b and e displays the effect of heating time on the PLQY and yield of S-CDs respectively. The PLQY and yield reaches the maximum (95 ± 5%) at the time of 5 h. In addition, the PLQY of S-CDs is closely related to the composition of precursors. Fig. 2c and f shows the R CA / EDA dependent PLQY and yield respectively, where the R CA / EDA was changed from 0.5/9.5 to 7/3. The highest PLQY appears at R CA/EDA = 1/9 while highest yield appears at R CA/EDA = 4/6. From the above results, one can see increasing the synthesis temperature (>170 °C) or prolonging the reaction time (>5 h) can increase the carbonization degree of CDs, but contributes little to enhancing the PLQY. In other words, carbonization/graphitization degree contributes little to the PLQY of the S-CDs.

Fig. 3 a and b exhibit a three-dimensional and a two-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the S-CDs respectively. The excitation region is mainly located at 300–400 nm and the emission region is mainly concentrated at 400–550 nm. Fig. 3c shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (purple), excitation spectrum (blue), emission spectrum (multi-color) for the S-CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum has two main absorption bands. The peak near 360 nm is a characteristic absorption peak, which is almost in coincide with the excitation peak of the S-CDs. The absorption peak may be attributed to the
n → π
*
electronic transitions of the functional groups on the surface of S-CDs, such as –NH–, –NH2 or other groups, which are likely to be the component of the fluorephore. The excitation peak and emission peak are almost symmetrical, located at 360 nm and 441 nm respectively. In addition, the emission peak is filled with multicolor according to the wavelength and one can see the center of the peak is in pure blue. Furthermore, we investigated the emission behavior response to excitation wavelength, where the emission spectra of S-CDs were measured by changing the excitation wavelength in the range of 300–400 nm. For easy observation, these emission spectra were normalized as shown in Fig. 3d. One can see that these normalized emission spectra almost coincide, which are independent of excitation wavelengths, implying that the S-CDs might have an exclusive PL emission center or fluorophore [24–26].

The morphology of S-CDs provides direct evidence that they are not real carbon dots. Fig. 3e displays a TEM image of the S-CDs, where several particles with different size distribution are observed, ranging from nanometer to micrometer. These features are not in line with the conventional morphology and size of CDs (<10 nm). In addition, one cannot observe many particles under the TEM, implying these particles might be not the only substance with PL activity. Fig. 3f shows a zoom-in TEM image of a particle in 2 nm resolution, where no ordered lattice could be observed. This phenomenon suggests that these particles not only have an inconsistent size distribution with conventional CDs, but also do not have graphene cores. Furthermore, these particles may not be the only component of the S-CDs, which may also contain many copolymers of CA and EDA.

Herein, the stability of the S-CDs was tested vs different NaCl concentration, pH, temperature, and solvents. The PL intensity of the S-CDs changes little when the concentration of NaCl gradually increased from 0 mM to 200 mM (Fig. 4 a and b), suggesting that the S-CDs have good salt resistance and keep considerable PL ability even in a relatively high concentration of NaCl solution. Fig. 4c and d shows the PL emission of the S-CDs response to pH. It is clear that the S-CDs maintain considerable PL intensity in the pH = 4–11, indicating that the S-CD can be used in a wide pH range. Then, the effect of temperature on the PL performance of S-CDs was examined as shown in Fig. 4e and f. With the increase of temperature at 20–60 °C, the PL intensity of the S-CDs decreases slightly. When the temperature rises to 60 °C, the S-CDs can still maintain ∼84% of the PL intensity at 20 °C. It means that the S-CDs can be safely used below 60 °C. Furthermore, we studied the PL stability of the S-CDs in different solvent enviroments. Six common solvents in the laboratory, secondary distilled water (WAT), ethanol (ETH), methanol (MET), acetone (ACE), tetrahydrofuran (DMF), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), were chosen to prepare the S-CDs solutions with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL, and their fluorescence spectra were measured as shown in Fig. 4g. The PL intensity of the S-CDs in the six solvents is in the order of: ETH > WAT > MET > DMF > ACE > THF. Even in THF, S-CDs can still maintain about 70% of the PL intensity in aqueous solution, indicating that S-CDs can adapt to most solvent environments. The above results suggest that the S-CDs are an excellent fluorescent material that can be used in a variety of harsh environments.

If S-CDs have an exclusive PL center, the PLQY of S-CDs should be related to the number of PL centers rather than the size of S-CDs. In other words, small particles (<1 nm) or intermediates with PL centers should also have PL ability. In order to verify this inference, a dialysis experiment was designed for the product S-CDs solution, which was synthesized under the condition of R CA/EDA = 4/6, total amount n = 10 mmol, T = 170 °C, t = 5 h. The as prepared product solution was loaded into a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 1000 Da) and dialyzed for 48 h in secondary distilled water as illustrated in Fig. 5 a and b. The absorbance dependent integral PL intensities of the product solution, retention solution and exudate are displayed in Fig. 5c, and their PLQYs are shown in Fig. 5d. The PLQYs of the product solution, retention solution and exudate are PLQY = 85 ± 2%, 74.5 ± 3% and 86.1 ± 1%, respectively. It is clear that the exudate also has PL ability. The dialysis bag has a cut-off molecular weight of 1000 Da. So only small molecules with molecular weight less than 1000 Da can permeate through dialysis membrane and enter the bulk solution. It implies that the intermediates or small molecules also have PL ability. One would be surprised why the exudate has higher PLQY value than the retention solution. It means that the intermediates or small molecules have stronger PL ability than these large particles inside the dialysis bag. Obviously, these large particles have higher carbonization degree, which also confirms that the PL ability is independent of carbonization degree. Furthermore, after drying the exudate and retention solution for a long enough time, one cannot obtain dry powder but honey like viscous substances. This observation confirms that the S-CDs might essentially be low molecular weight copolymers (or polymer dots, PDs) produced by the reaction of CA and EDA, which are obviously different from traditional carbon QDs or graphene QDs.

The PL mechanism of S-CDs remains unclear. It is well known that most CDs synthesized by pure carbohydrates exhibit poor PLQY while nitrogen doping can effectively improve the PLQY of carbon dots. Especially. The CDs synthesized by CA and EDA exhibit excellent PL performance with PLQY up to ∼100%. Our experimental results suggest that a heating temperature of 170 °C and a heating time of 5 h are the optimal synthesis condition of the S-CDs, which obviously cannot effectively lead to full carbonization of precursors, but might be favorable for the synthesis of their copolymers. For a spherical copolymer, its size can reach 6 nm when the molecular weight is ∼3000 Da. The excitation wavelength independent emission behavior suggests that the S-CDs have an exclusive PL center or fluorophore. Since amino group has lone-pair electrons, easy to complex with metal ions and cause the fluorescence quenching of the S-CDs, the PL center of the S-CDs is closely related to amino group. A multinuclear solid state nuclear magnetic resonance study [27] reveals that 1,2,3,5-tetrahydroimidazo [1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid (IPCA) might be the PL center/fluorophore of the S-CDs. Therefore, the possible reaction mechanism of S-CDs is illustrated in Fig. 6 . This explains why increasing the synthesis temperature (>170 °C) or prolonging the reaction time (>5 h) can enhance the carbonization degree of S-CDs, but contributes little to enhancing the PLQY. This observation indicates that the PLQY has little relationship with the carbonization degree, but is related to the number of PL centers.

In summary, although the fluorescent CDs synthesized by CA and EDA have excellent fluorescence performance, they behave differently from conventional CDs. Carbonization/graphitization degree contributes little to the PLQY of S-CDs, which is closely related to the molar ratio of CA/EDA. Morphological characterization shows the S-CDs have no graphene cores. Excitation wavelength independent emission behavior implies that the S-CDs might have an exclusive PL center, which is independent of the size of the S-CDs (quantum size effect). Dialysis experiment reveals that not only nanoparticles, small molecular fragments also have PL ability. All of our experimental observations reveal that the S-CDs should be not real CDs but copolymer of CA and EDA. Combining the results from the literatures, a possible reaction mechanism was proposed. It can be inferred from this example that many CDs with high PLQY are molecular CDs with fluorephore. This work sheds light on the essence of S-CDs and paves the way for their large-scale industrial production and applications.

Mengna Zhang: Sample synthesis, Spectra acquisition, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiang Long: Sample synthesis, Spectra acquisition, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yanshun Ma: Conceptualization, Review, Methodology, Project administration. Shaogui Wu: Review, Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
